text: This is a robotic assisted left floral lobectomy.
And initially we start out and we mobilize the inferior pulmonary ligament and we look for any station nine lymph nodes.
And visualized here is a station nine lymph node being removed.
This is lower lobectomy, so we will skeletonize the inferior pulmonary vein.
And once we've done that, we then go to the anterior mediastinum.
dissecting out between the inferior pulmonary vein and the fissure.
And this is the anterior mediastinum with the superior pulmonary vein.
And this is going to be a station 5 lymph node, which we remove.
And we're working clockwise around.
So we'll take out station 5 and we'll look up for station 6 lymph nodes.
And we mobilize the apex of the lung.
So the apex of the lung is being retracted inferiorly.
And we begin to section between the pulmonary artery and the aorta and sometimes you'll find a station six lymph node there and you send that for specimen.
Then we proceed around again clockwise to the posterior mediastinum and we'll follow the left lower lobe bronchus up.
then find some station 7 lymph nodes, and then we'll dissect the pulmonary artery as it comes down into the fissure.
And frequently you can see the posterior segmental branch to the upper lobe and the superior segmental branch to the lower lobe.
We then go into the fissure and you find your pulmonary artery in the fissure.
And if you dissect it out, your mediastinum, anterior and posterior, it makes the fissure very easy.
The fissure now has been divided.
This is a station 11 lymph node being removed.
And now we're continuing in the fissure, removing our hilar and interlobar lymph nodes.
And then we're going to separate the superior segmental fissure from the posterior segmental fissure.
This is a vessel loop going around the fissure.
We're going to bring in our robotic green 30 stapler with an anvil tip.
We set our angle and then we come in and we fire our stapler and this now is going to expose the hilum and expose the lower lobe segmental arteries.
This is one we're able to go around the lower lobe but we can also get the superior segmental branch as well.
We'll again use a vessel loop and then through the left hand we're going to come in with a robotic
white stapler with anvil tip.
It always comes anvil up.
You rotate it, clutch your hand, get it in a comfortable position, set your angle and bring it in.
It's got a nice smooth tip to where it won't graze the vessel as it comes through.
The lower lobe branch is now divided.
We're now going to retract the lung superiorly and isolate the inferior pulmonary vein, again, using a vessel loop.
And you notice I always have a cigar in the field just in case I get any adverse bleeding.
I can tamponade it with the cigar.
Again, we now will retract up on the inferior pulmonary vein.
Through my left hand, the white stapler comes in.
We rotate it to the correct position.
Bring it into the vessel set and fire.
Once we've divided the inferior pulmonary vein, now all we have left is the bronchus.
So again, we go around the bronchus.
I like to use vessel loops.
Again, coming in through the left hand.
For the lower lobe, most of these can be done with a stapler anteriorly or in the left hand.
With an upper lobe, having one posteriorly helps as well.
I'm still old school.
I'll clamp the bronchus, inflate the lung, make sure the upper lobe inflates normally.
And now the lower lobectomy is complete.
We fill the chest full of saline, inflate the lung looking for an air leak.
In the fissure we see a little air leak which I cauterize and later after we've removed the fluid I'll come in and we use Progel pulmonary sealant just to ensure that they have no air leak.
Once we have sealed the leak we now bring in a chest tube, a 20 French chest tube done under direct vision with the robot.
And then once that is secured to the skin, then we bring a specimen bag in to put the specimen in.
And we use either a large anchor bag, a 15 millimeter or 1500 milliliter bag, or a 12 millimeter, 1200 milliliter Cavidian or Medtronic bag.
Now that specimen's going in the specimen bag, we're now going to externalize it.
I'll go subcostal through the diaphragm, and usually you'll end up like a two inch place in the diaphragm, which we repair with a 2-0 monofilament V-lock suture.
And again, it's easy to sew.
Most of the time I'll use a needle driver on my right hand, although occasionally I'll just continue to use the long bipolar grasper.
And once it is complete, we cut the stitch and externalize.
